title: The Disappearing Overnight Drift - Liberty Street Economics
gist: The 'overnight drift'—a ~3.7% annualized return from holding S&P 500 E-mini futures in the 2:00–3:00 a.m. ET window around the European open—has faded to roughly zero since 2021, and the NY Fed authors attribute the collapse almost entirely to a halving of end-of-day order-imbalance dispersion (likely from algorithmic liquidity providers slicing flow more finely), while volatility and overnight liquidity are essentially unchanged.

themes.theme: The Fade of the Overnight Drift

insights.claim: The overnight drift in U.S. equity futures—a large return earned holding S&P 500 E-mini positions between 2:00 and 3:00 a.m. ET around the European open—that previously generated roughly 3.7 percent per annum has averaged close to zero since 2021.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: A once-tradable overnight equity-futures anomaly has died, so strategies systematically buying the U.S. close and selling the open no longer carry an edge.
insights.quote: the 2:00–3:00 window that previously generated roughly 3.7 percent per annum has averaged close to zero since 2021
insights.locator: In a 2021 Liberty Street Economics post

insights.claim: The 2:00–3:00 a.m. ET window was previously responsible for more than 60 percent of the S&P 500 E-mini's 5.9 percent annualized close-to-close return, but is flat in the January 2021–December 2025 sample.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Most of the futures contract's historical total return came from a single overnight hour that has now disappeared, materially reshaping where intraday returns accrue.
insights.quote: previously responsible for more than 60 percent of the contract’s 5.9 percent annualized close-to-close return—is flat
insights.locator: The next chart repeats the exercise

insights.claim: The disappearance of the overnight drift holds not just in the S&P 500 E-mini but also in the E-mini Nasdaq-100 (NQ) and E-mini Dow Jones (YM) contracts, indicating a market-wide rather than contract-specific change.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: The cross-contract signature means the fade reflects a structural shift in U.S. equity-index microstructure, not an idiosyncratic quirk of one contract.
insights.quote: The same pattern holds in the E-mini Nasdaq-100 (NQ) and E-mini Dow Jones (YM) contracts.
insights.locator: The next chart repeats the exercise

insights.claim: NightShares launched two overnight-return ETFs (NSPY and NIWM) in June 2022 that cited the inventory-risk mechanism and were closed fourteen months later, consistent with the weakened drift pattern.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: A real-money product built explicitly to harvest the overnight drift failed within about a year, a live confirmation that the anomaly stopped paying.
insights.quote: Both funds were closed fourteen months later, consistent with the weakened pattern over this sample (though ETF-specific costs likely also contributed).
insights.locator: An episode from an asset management company

insights.claim: The overnight drift is compensation earned by liquidity providers who absorb one-sided institutional flow at the U.S. close, carry risky inventory overnight in thin markets, and earn back the discount they demanded when overseas buyers arrive at the Frankfurt and London open.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Frames overnight equity returns as a risk-premium paid to inventory-bearing market makers, so the return only persists while closing imbalances force large discounts.
insights.quote: When institutional flows tilt heavily to one side in the final hour of trading, LPs step in as buyers of last resort and carry the resulting inventory overnight.
insights.locator: The Overnight Drift in One Equation

insights.claim: In equilibrium the expected overnight return equals the product of three observable factors: dollar order imbalance, return variance, and the inverse of liquidity providers' risk-bearing capacity, so the drift collapses if any one factor collapses.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Gives investors a decomposable, monitorable equation—track order imbalance, VIX, and dealer capacity—to know when overnight drift-type premia should reappear.
insights.locator: As we derive in our paper

insights.claim: Because overnight markets are thin, even modest incoming order flow moves prices substantially, which amplifies the rebound that constitutes the overnight drift.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Explains why the effect concentrated in a single thin-liquidity window and why liquidity conditions are a necessary input to the premium.
insights.quote: Because overnight markets are thin, even modest incoming order flow moves prices substantially, amplifying the rebound.
insights.locator: The Overnight Drift in One Equation

insights.claim: The standard deviation of end-of-day relative signed volume (net buyer-initiated share of last-hour 15:15–16:15 ET volume) fell from a full-sample average of 6.5 percent to 2.9 percent, a compression of more than half.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Quantifies the single input the authors blame for the drift's disappearance—extreme one-sided closes have become far rarer.
insights.quote: the standard deviation of end-of-day RSV fell from 6.5 percent to 2.9 percent—a compression of more than half
insights.locator: The first channel is the magnitude

insights.claim: The predictive relationship between closing order imbalances and next-day overnight returns has broken down: large negative closing imbalances predicted positive overnight returns in 2007–20 but the spread across imbalance bins is much narrower in 2021–25.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: The signal that end-of-day flow gave about overnight direction has decayed, removing a systematic timing input for overnight positioning.
insights.quote: In 2021–25 (bottom panel), the spread is much narrower, consistent with a weaker predictive link in the post-publication sample.
insights.locator: The compression shows up in the predictive relationship

insights.claim: Limit orders posted at the close have become smaller in the post-2021 sample, consistent with algorithmic liquidity providers slicing flow more finely and transmitting less residual inventory onto end-of-day counterparties.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Points to algorithmic execution as the structural driver eroding the anomaly, suggesting the fade is durable unless execution regimes reverse.
insights.quote: limit orders posted at the close have become smaller in the post-publication sample, consistent with algorithmic liquidity providers slicing flow more finely

insights.claim: The VIX barely moved across samples, with its mean falling only modestly from 20.4 to 19.4 (medians 18.6 versus 18.2), ruling out declining return variance as the cause of the drift's disappearance.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Since volatility is stable, investors cannot attribute the vanished premium to a calmer market regime that could later reverse.
insights.quote: its mean fell only modestly from 20.4 in the original sample to 19.4 in the post-publication period, with median values of 18.6 and 18.2
insights.locator: Volatility and Liquidity Have Barely Moved

insights.claim: Overnight liquidity is essentially unchanged, with the overnight share of total E-mini volume edging up only modestly from 15 percent to 16 percent between the pre-2020 and post-2020 samples.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Isolates order-imbalance compression as the lone shifting input, since liquidity provision capacity did not change.
insights.quote: the overnight share of total E-mini volume has edged up only modestly, from 15 percent to 16 percent
insights.locator: Volatility and Liquidity Have Barely Moved

insights.claim: The framework yields a falsifiable prediction: if end-of-day order-imbalance dispersion widens back toward its pre-2020 range, the overnight drift should reappear in the same 2:00–3:00 a.m. ET window with the same cross-contract signature.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete trigger to watch—rising closing-imbalance dispersion—for redeploying an overnight-drift strategy.
insights.quote: if order-imbalance dispersion widens back toward its pre-2020 range, the overnight drift should reappear in the same 2:00–3:00 window, with the same cross-contract signature
insights.locator: Our framework yields a falsifiable prediction
